preamble: The voiced palatal lateral approximant /ʎ/ can undergo a historical phonetic transformation to the voiced postalveolar affricate /d͡ʒ/. This shift typically involves a process of fortition, where the approximant strengthens to an affricate, often passing through an intermediate fricative stage. It also entails delateralization and a slight backing of the place of articulation from palatal to postalveolar.
phoneticEffects: Fortition, affrication, delateralization, backing (palatal to postalveolar)

examples.note: In some dialects of Spanish, the historical palatal lateral /ʎ/ (e.g., in words spelled with <ll>) has undergone fortition, often via an intermediate palatal fricative /ʝ/, to a postalveolar affricate /d͡ʒ/ or alveolo-palatal affricate /d͡ʑ/. This phenomenon is part of 'rehilamiento' in certain varieties, such as those found in parts of Latin America and Southern Spain.
In some Sardinian varieties, the Latin cluster /lj/ (which developed into a palatal lateral in many Romance languages) evolved into a geminate voiced postalveolar affricate.

sources: Maiden, M. (2008). Lenition revisited. Journal of Linguistics, 44(3), 565-602.
Zampaulo, A. (2013). When Synchrony Meets Diachrony: (Alveolo)Palatal Sound Patterns In Spanish and Other Romance Languages. The Ohio State University.

Zampaulo, A. (2022). An Auditory Based, Apparent Time Analysis of Palatal Strengthening in Medellín Spanish: Sociolinguistic Predictors of a Phonetic Change in Progress. Hispanic Studies Review, 7(1), 101-125.
